sourceMovie.title: JFK
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto JFK (1991) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Confessions of a Dangerous Mind
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Confessions of a Dangerous Mind draws from the same drama well as JFK, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2002 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Shooter
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Shooter (2007) captures the drama appeal of JFK without simply imitating it. The thriller streak running through both films is what puts Shooter at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: In the Line of Fire
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama appeal of JFK — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into In the Line of Fire (1993). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Edge of Darkness
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Edge of Darkness (2010) clearly understood what makes drama work — the same quality that keeps JFK rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Syriana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Syriana (2005) is the kind of film JFK fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Arlington Road
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Arlington Road shares JFK's gift for drama — the 1999 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Parkland
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes JFK compelling — the drama, the history — shows up in Parkland (2013) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that tense storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: The Parallax View
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The drama energy of JFK is alive in The Parallax View (1974), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Driven
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Driven (2019) operates in the same thriller as JFK, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Taxi Driver
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Taxi Driver (1976) belongs to the same conversation as JFK — both anchor their drama in crime emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

faq.question: What should I watch after JFK?
faq.answer: Try Confessions of a Dangerous Mind, Shooter, In the Line of Fire—each shares DNA with JFK while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is JFK?
faq.answer: JFK (1991) is best described as Drama, Thriller, History. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is JFK worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
